findings.message: The Phase 2 classification for `S ∩ C` conflicts with the skip rule below it: the table marks structural matches as `🔵`, but the later `skip` rule says `action=Reference` + structural equality should be suppressed instead of reported. As written, the same case is both a blue signal and a skip.

`D3` says this is a `10 領域` table, but the table itself enumerates 11 distinct conditions, and the consequence note repeats the same `10 領域` claim. The count is internally inconsistent; either the heading/bullets need to say 11 or one row needs to be merged.

`transition_methods` is allowed to reference `trait_impls`内の `MethodDeclaration` here, but D10 later removes `methods` from `TraitImplDecl` entirely. That leaves no schema location for the referenced method declarations, so the typestate transition path is internally inconsistent as written.

The Phase 1 `Delete` step removes an item from `S` but never rewrites or rejects surviving references to that item. For any deletion of a still-referenced baseline item, `S` will end up with dangling `Id`s, so the stated fully-resolved `S` cannot actually be constructed.

ADR 1 says the signal evaluator refines action semantics into a "10-region" table, but the referenced ADR 3 D3 defines an 11-region table and repeats that count later. Because ADR 1 explicitly points readers to ADR 3 D3 as the canonical region definition, this cross-reference leaves implementers with conflicting signal-table cardinality.

This assigns implicit Reference resolution for undeclared baseline-derived items to the codec, but ADR 2 D10 keeps the A codec open-world and defers resolution to S construction, while ADR 3 D4 says the signal evaluator dynamically attaches Reference to B. That is a component-boundary contradiction and can lead to implementing baseline-dependent behavior in the catalogue codec.

D is defined as the set of B-derived items declared for deletion, but the pseudocode adds the A/catalogue element to D (`D.add(elt rebuild_id)`) rather than the removed baseline item from S/B. Following this literally makes D contain the delete declaration shape instead of the baseline item, which contradicts the D definition and can corrupt delete progress evaluation and D-specific external_crates rebuilding.

The signal distribution summary omits `S \ C + action=Add`, even though the table immediately above classifies that region as Yellow. This makes the summary inconsistent with the canonical table and risks dropping the new-declare-not-yet-implemented case from implementations that follow the summary.

ADR 3 defines the Phase 2 table as 11 regions, but ADR 1 still references a '10-region' signal table. That leaves the catalogue ADR pointing at the wrong evaluator contract for action semantics; update the ADR 1 D4/Related text to match ADR 3's 11-region table, or adjust ADR 3 if 10 was intended.

The canonical schema prose introduces `ItemAction`, and ADR 2/3 consume `ItemAction`, but the ADR 1 schema snippets declare `action: TypeAction` for TypeEntry/TraitEntry/FunctionEntry. This is a semantic type-name mismatch in the plan artifacts and will send implementers toward a non-selected action type.

This states `S.external_crates` is the full union of B and A, but the same paragraph says deletes can remove dependencies from S and leave them only in D. A full B∪A union cannot drop a baseline-only dependency used solely by a deleted item, so the S/D construction contract is internally inconsistent and may make Phase 2 compare against the wrong target graph.

D is defined as the set of B-derived items being deleted, but the Delete branch iterates over A and adds `elt` to D. That contradicts the definition and can lose the baseline item's actual structure/references; the branch should make clear that D receives the matched baseline/S item removed by the delete operation, not the catalogue delete declaration itself.

ADR 2 cites its own D6 as the decision establishing '1 catalogue = 1 crate', but D6 is about fixed values for rustdoc fields; the crate-per-catalogue decision is ADR 1 D6. This broken decision reference appears in the codec rationale and rejected alternative text, so readers are directed to the wrong design decision.

D1 says the Layer axis is determined implicitly from catalogue file placement, but D6 defines an explicit `layer: Layer` field inside `CatalogueDocument` and says the document declares its layer. This leaves the schema contract ambiguous: implementers cannot tell whether layer is derived from path/layout or parsed from the JSON field.

D1 states that A/S extend `rustdoc_types::Crate` with action only and that role/pattern/docs remain on the Catalogue side, but D7 later says catalogue `docs` are encoded into rustdoc `Item.docs`. Those two rules conflict over whether docs are part of the TypeGraph produced by the codec.

The Related section still describes the signal evaluator ADR as a future planned ADR, while this ADR already references the concrete ADR 3 decisions earlier and the scoped change includes that ADR. This makes the cross-document relationship stale and self-contradictory; it should point to `2026-05-08-0305-tddd-signal-evaluator-three-way-diff.md` as the sibling/follow-up ADR already present.

ADR 1 D11 explicitly uses `FunctionPath` because function short names such as `new`/`build` commonly collide, but ADR 2 makes graph identity `name`-only. That means two allowed catalogue functions with the same short name in different modules collapse during matching/Id rebuild, so the plan cannot correctly evaluate a catalogue shape that ADR 1 says is valid.

The external-crate construction rule contradicts itself: this passage says `S.external_crates` is rebuilt only from items remaining after Delete, so delete-only dependencies disappear from S, but the same ADR's pseudocode and Reassess section describe `S.external_crates = B ∪ A`, and ADR 2 D5 says to union B and A external crates. Those two rules produce different S graphs whenever a deleted baseline item was the only user of an external crate.

ADR 1 says undeclared baseline-derived entries are auto-resolved as implicit References by the codec, but ADR 2/3 place that responsibility in the signal evaluator: A codec is open-world and independent of B, while Phase 1 builds S from B with dynamic `Reference` attachment. Leaving this as-is gives conflicting ownership for whether the codec may read/resolve baseline items.

ADR 2 now defines identity as short name for types/traits but full FunctionPath for functions, while ADR 3 Phase 1 still says all identity matching and Id rebuilds are by name. That makes functions like different `new`/`build` entries collide or be matched incorrectly in S/D/C construction, contradicting ADR 1 D11 and ADR 2 D3.

The signal table classifies `S \ C + action=Add` as Yellow, but the immediately following Yellow distribution omits that region. If implementers use the distribution summary, declared-but-unimplemented adds can be dropped from Yellow reporting despite being part of the canonical table.

D1/D7 state that catalogue docs are encoded into `rustdoc Item.docs`, but D2 says `role / pattern / docs` remain only on `CatalogueDocument`. This leaves the TypeGraph/codec contract ambiguous about whether docs are present in A/S at all, despite render/documentation being listed as a consumer.

D11 says unprefixed TypeRef identifiers always mean same-catalogue declarations, but the ADR examples rely on unprefixed standard/prelude types such as `Result`, `Option`, `Vec`, and `String`. With the stated open-world/closed-world flow, those become unresolved markers and then Phase 1 errors unless the catalogue/baseline declares them, so common declared TypeRefs are physically unexecutable without an additional std/prelude resolution rule or prefix requirement.

`bool` is listed with `Vec`/`Option`/`String` as a prefix-less std item that the codec should resolve as a `"std"` external-crate reference. That contradicts the `rustdoc_types` mapping described in D9, where Rust primitives should become `Type::Primitive`; encoding `bool` as a resolved external path will make catalogue-derived A structurally differ from rustdoc-derived B/C for valid fields such as `active: bool`. Split primitive handling from std-prelude path handling.

D2 states immediately above that functions are identified by `FunctionPath`, but the action branches still do `assert elt in S (by name)` / `get_by_name`. If implemented literally, duplicate function names in different modules, which ADR 1 D11 explicitly supports via `FunctionPath`, become ambiguous during Add/Modify/Reference/Delete processing. The pseudocode needs to use the same identity key as the surrounding text: short name for types/traits, full FunctionPath for functions.

ADR 3 narrows function identity to `FunctionPath (module_path + name)`, but ADR 2 D3 defines `FunctionPath = crate_name + module_path + name` and says all segments are used. If the signal evaluator follows ADR 3, it can build identity keys that do not match the catalogue key shape from ADR 1/2, and same-module/name functions from different crates would collapse whenever graphs are aggregated. Keep the crate_name segment in the ADR 3 identity rule or explicitly state why this evaluator is strictly single-crate and how the full catalogue key is normalized.

The Delete branch rejects as soon as the deleted item is still referenced by any current S item, but the same Phase 1 loop also allows Modify to replace items. A valid change that deletes `OldType` and modifies `User` to remove its `OldType` field can be rejected if Delete is processed before Modify, making the algorithm order-dependent and contradicting the stated goal that S is the final result of applying A to B. The plan needs to apply Add/Modify replacements before dangling-reference validation, or run that validation after final S construction.

D11 now says prefixless prelude types like `Vec`, `Option`, `Result`, `String`, and `Box` are resolved as a `std` external crate, but D5 still defines `external_crates` extraction as only `TraitImplDecl.origin_crate` plus crate-prefixed `TypeRef`s. A catalogue using `Vec<UserId>` would therefore create a `ResolvedPath` to `std` without the corresponding `external_crates` entry/crate_id, making the codec rules internally inconsistent.

Phase 1 checks for dangling IDs before resolving A's unresolved TypeRef markers, then resolves those markers against raw B. If a catalogue Add/Modify references a baseline item that is also Delete-declared, the pre-resolution dangling check cannot see that reference, and `B.find_by_name` can still resolve it after the item has been moved to D. That either leaves S referencing a deleted item or reintroduces it, contradicting the stated requirement that references to deleted items be rejected as a Phase 1 Error. The marker resolution needs to run before the dangling-reference validation, or resolve only against the post-delete S universe.

ADR 3 says Delete処理後の S is the closed-world universe used to reject references to deleted types, but the same paragraph also resolves against `A.index`. Since A still contains catalogue items including Delete declarations, a reference to a deleted item can resolve through A after it was removed from S, accepting the exact case this section says must become a Phase 1 Error. Remove the A fallback or restrict it to non-delete items already retained in S.

ADR 2 still defines unresolved marker resolution as `B (Baseline) + A` and says the universe is `B + A`, while ADR 3 now defines the closed-world universe as post-delete S. Following ADR 2 would allow deleted baseline items from B to resolve unresolved TypeRefs, contradicting ADR 3's delete-after-S invariant and defeating deleted-reference rejection. Update D10 and the matching consequence text to use the post-delete S rule.

Phase 1.5 says unresolved markers must resolve only against the post-Delete S universe so references to deleted types are rejected, but the pseudocode still falls back to `A.find_by_name(...)`. Because `A` still contains Delete declarations, this branch can resolve a marker to an item that was intentionally removed from S, contradicting the stated closed-world rule and allowing the exact deleted-type reference the plan says must fail.

This consequence still says Phase 1 rejects markers that cannot resolve in `B + A`, while D10 now defines the resolver as post-Delete S only. That stale universe definition conflicts with the updated Delete handling and would preserve deleted declarations as valid resolution targets if implemented from this summary.

Rejected alternative H claims ADR 1 D10 adopted optional trait impl methods, but ADR 1 D10 actually removes `TraitImplDecl.methods` entirely and the next sentence says the same. This self-contradiction leaves the rejected-alternative rationale pointing at a decision state that no longer exists.

[TypeRef](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/domain-types.json#L221) is documented as rejecting `::` module-path separators, but T005 requires extracting crate prefixes from `TypeRef` to auto-build `external_crates` ([plan.md](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/plan.md#L32)) and [spec.json](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/spec.json#L173) explicitly depends on that prefix-based extraction. As written, the codec cannot represent any external type path.

`SignalRegion` is described as encoding 11 evaluation regions, but the enum lists 12 variants ([domain-types.json](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/domain-types.json#L1062)). That clashes with the repeated `11 regions` wording in [spec.json](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/spec.json#L193) and [plan.md](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/plan.md#L42), so T007 does not have a single unambiguous table shape.

The spec says Phase 2 uses a "12 領域 × signal table" while citing ADR 3 D3, but the implementation plan for T007 correctly says ADR 3 D3 is an 11-region table. The same 12-region wording is repeated for IN-12 and AC-08, so the plan artifacts disagree on the required evaluator coverage and tests. Either align the spec to the ADR/plan's 11 logical regions, or explicitly distinguish 12 enum variants from 11 logical regions as domain-types.json does.

T004 requires implementing a concrete `BaselineRustdocCodec`, but the infrastructure type catalogue and contract map only define `BaselineRustdocCodecError`; the secondary-adapter list includes `CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodec`, `SignalEvaluatorV2`, and `FsCatalogueLoader`, with no baseline codec node or SOT chain. This leaves a task-required implementation outside the catalogue/contract artifacts, so the plan is not traceable: either add the adapter entry and its contract edges/spec refs, or remove the codec from T004 if raw `rustdoc_types::Crate` deserialization is intended.

IN-12 says Phase 2 covers `S ∩ C` only for `構造一致 + action別`, but AC-08 and T007 require handling `S ∩ C + 構造不一致` as a distinct signal region. This makes the canonical spec internally inconsistent and can lead implementers to omit the mismatch rows from the 3-way table despite the acceptance test requiring them.

T006 is ordered before T007 and is required to implement `SignalEvaluatorPort` returning `Result<ThreeWayEvaluationReport, Phase1Error>`, but T007 is the task that introduces `ThreeWayEvaluationReport` and the related Phase 2 report types. With the stated sequential dependency `T006 完了 → T007 着手`, T006 cannot complete the port as described without a type that the plan defers to the next task.

`BaselineRustdocCodec` is introduced as a filesystem-loading `secondary_adapter`, but its catalogue entry has `implements: []` and the contract map shows no port edge for it. That leaves the new Baseline/Current rustdoc JSON dependency outside the trait boundary, unlike the other new adapters, so the plan is not hexagonal/traceable for B/C loading. Add a secondary port for this loader or reclassify it as an infrastructure-internal helper instead of an adapter contract item.

T004 requires implementing a concrete `BaselineRustdocCodec`, but the infrastructure catalogue only declares `BaselineRustdocCodecError` and the contract map has no `BaselineRustdocCodec` adapter node or edge. That leaves a task-required implementation outside the plan artifacts' SOT chain, so the implementation plan is not traceable for the baseline/current rustdoc loader.

T008 is ordered before the catalogue rewrite but still requires `cargo make ci` to be green. By this point T003 has removed the old `TypeCatalogueDocument` read path, while T009 later says the existing `*-types.json` files must still be converted because the new codec cannot read the old schema. That makes the T008 CI gate infeasible; the catalogue rewrite has to happen before the first full CI/verify gate that uses the new codec.

`CatalogueLinter` is kept as an unmodified reference port whose signature still takes `&TypeCatalogueDocument`, but the same catalogue marks `TypeCatalogueDocument` for deletion. Once the old schema is removed, this referenced port cannot remain unchanged, so the plan contradicts itself about whether old catalogue schema consumers are migrated.

The codec error catalogue says crate-prefixed TypeRef values can fail as `UnknownCrateName`, but the same plan requires external_crates to be auto-built from every TraitImplDecl origin crate and TypeRef crate prefix. Under ADR 2 D5/IN-10, a prefix like `domain_core::UserId` is supposed to be collected and assigned a per-graph crate_id, not rejected as unresolved. If implemented as written, valid cross-crate references would be treated as codec errors.

`CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodecError` adds `ExternalCrateUnresolvable`, but the plan/spec says the A codec is open-world: crate-prefixed and std-allowlisted references are auto-collected into `external_crates`, while unresolved catalogue-local references are carried as markers and rejected later by Phase 1. The domain port also returns `NewTypeGraphCodecError`, whose catalogue entry explicitly limits codec errors to parse/ambiguity cases. This extra codec-level rejection path contradicts the declared TypeRef/external_crates semantics and would either be unreachable or force the adapter to violate the port contract.

The usecase catalogue still marks both reference ports as cat-spec yellow, but the briefing says this layer should be 2 blue / 0 yellow / 0 red. That leaves the usecase signal summary out of sync with the artifact.

The domain catalogue now has more than the single accepted yellow: `ContractMapWriter`, `CatalogueLinter`, and `SchemaExporter` are cat-spec yellow in addition to the intended `TypeKindV2` yellow. That contradicts the briefing's 57 blue / 1 yellow / 0 red summary and means the domain yellow budget is exceeded.

This entry says the contract-map renderer update for the new TypeGraph format is out of scope, but the spec includes the required `contract_map_render` rewrite in IN-13/AC-09 and OS-06 only defers detailed TypeKind/Role rendering. If implementers follow this catalogue wording, T008 can skip the renderer migration and fail the planned acceptance criteria.

AC-07 says Phase 1 takes `A + B + C` to build `S` and `D`, but the rest of the plan and ADR 3 split this as Phase 1 = `A + B` construction and Phase 2 = `S / D / C` comparison. This acceptance criterion can drive the wrong tests or implementation by making `C` a Phase 1 dependency when it should only be used in Phase 2.

`ThreeWayEvaluationReport` is described as containing signals for “all evaluated items,” but ADR 3 D3 defines the `S ∩ C + Reference + match` region as `skip` specifically to reduce output by not emitting those maintained items. As written, the report contract would include skipped reference matches, contradicting the intended signal output semantics.

`CatalogueDocumentError` declares `DuplicateTypeName` / `DuplicateTraitName` / `DuplicateFunctionPath`, but the document is modeled directly as `BTreeMap`s. If the new codec deserializes straight into those maps, duplicate keys are collapsed before validation, so these errors cannot be observed as written unless you add a raw intermediate form or a custom duplicate-preserving parse step.

`InMemoryCatalogueLinter` is declared as a referenced adapter that is “not modified in this track,” but `domain-types.json` changes the `CatalogueLinter` port signature to take `&CatalogueDocument` and explicitly says the adapter's implements edge is preserved. Since an implementation of a modified trait must update its method signature, leaving this adapter as reference-only makes the plan internally inconsistent and can leave T008 with a stale `TypeCatalogueDocument` implementation after the old schema is deleted.

This transition is no longer status-only: `domain-types.json:281-307` adds a new `IdentifierError`, and `domain-types.md:13-14` promotes `CompositePattern`/`VariantPayload` to blue even though `plan.md:13` still leaves T002 as todo. That makes the rendered plan describe a later semantic state than the task graph actually permits.

`domain-types.md` now marks multiple T002 deliverables as Signal 🔵, starting with `CompositePattern`/`VariantPayload`, while `impl-plan.json` and `plan.md` still have T002 only `in_progress` and describe those same composite structures as the active task's remaining work. The plan artifacts now disagree about whether T002's semantic scope is already satisfied, which can mislead the T002 transition/review gate. Keep these rows yellow until T002 is actually completed, or complete T002 consistently with its commit metadata.

The transition marks T002 only as `in_progress`, but this generated catalogue already flips several T002 deliverables such as `CompositePattern`, `VariantPayload`, `FieldDecl`, `VariantDecl`, `TraitImplDeclV2`, `TypeEntry`, `TraitEntry`, `FunctionEntry`, and `CatalogueDocument` to blue. That contradicts the plan view, where those same domain types are still the work of T002, and makes the artifacts read as if T002 is already satisfied while it is only being started.

`domain-types.md` now marks the T002 composite-schema deliverables starting with `CompositePattern` as 🔵, while the plan artifacts still show T002 as the active in-progress task. For the stated T001-done/T002-start transition, these semantic signal changes make the artifacts assert that T002 outputs are already satisfied before T002 is completed; either keep these signals unchanged for this transition or advance the task state/briefing consistently.

The transient-state signal updates for T004 are incomplete: `ExtendedCrate` and `NewTypeGraphCodecError` are still marked `🟡`, but the briefing says T004-related types should already be `🔵` during this in-progress transition. That leaves the plan artifacts inconsistent about the current stage.

`ParamDeclaration` and `MethodDeclaration` are still marked `🟡`, but the briefing says these T002-related types should be `🔵` after the T002 commit. The artifact therefore contradicts its own stated post-T002 signal state.

`ParamDeclaration` is marked `🔵` here, but the review brief says it should remain `🟡` in this transient state alongside `MethodDeclaration`. That makes the signal table semantically inconsistent with the intended plan-artifact state.

T004 is now marked `done`, but the scoped type signal views still leave T004-owned deliverables yellow: `ExtendedCrate` and `NewTypeGraphCodecError` in `domain-types.md`, plus `CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodecError` and `BaselineRustdocCodecError` in `infrastructure-types.md`. That makes the plan artifacts contradict themselves about whether T004 is actually complete; either the signals need to be regenerated to blue for T004, or T004 should not be marked done yet.

The `domain-types.md` signal table flips two T005 rows against the briefing: `ParamDeclaration` is still shown as 🟡 even though the briefing says it is already blue, while `MethodDeclaration` is shown as 🔵 even though the briefing says it is still missing `docs`, `is_async`, `receiver`, and `returns` and should remain yellow.

The `ExtendedCrate` contract graph understates the documented API: the constructor edge is labeled `new(item_actions)` even though the schema records `new(krate, item_actions)`, and the other method edges only point to `ItemAction` instead of the `Crate`/`BTreeMap<Id, ItemAction>` types those accessors expose.

`CatalogueDocumentCodecError` is internally inconsistent: the description says `Json` is a `serde_json` error, but the modeled variant is unit (`payload_types: []`). Either the prose should stop claiming an embedded error, or the variant should carry the error payload.
`MethodDeclaration` is marked as 🟡 here, but the review briefing says this row should be 🔵 after the T005 transition. That is a direct status contradiction in the generated signal table.

`BaselineRustdocCodec` fully deserializes into the current `rustdoc_types::Crate` before checking `format_version`. That means rustdoc JSON from an unsupported schema can fail serde first and be reported as `Json` instead of `UnsupportedFormatVersion` (for example, older format versions before `Crate::target` was added lack the now-required `target` field). Since this codec is meant to reject format mismatches via `UnsupportedFormatVersion`, read/parse just `format_version` first, compare it with `FORMAT_VERSION`, and only then deserialize the full `Crate`.

New external crate prefixes are assigned a placeholder `u32::MAX - 1` during `parse_type_ref_str`, and the returned `Type::ResolvedPath` is never rewritten after the real crate id is registered. The first reference to any unseen crate (for example `serde::Serialize`) will therefore point at an id that does not exist in `external_crates`.

`assign_ids` stores type and trait short names in the same `HashMap<String, Id>` without checking for collisions. If a type and trait share the same short name, one silently overwrites the other, so the later encode pass writes both entries under a single `Id` instead of surfacing the documented `AmbiguousTypeName` error.

`resolved_path_type` builds `Type::ResolvedPath` with an empty `path` string for every impl target. Downstream consumers that render or inspect `Impl.for_` by path will see a blank target type, so inherent and trait impl blocks lose the name of the type they belong to.

`parse_type_ref_str` ignores the `u32` returned by `emit_external_crate` and always leaves the parsed `Type::ResolvedPath` pointing at `u32::MAX - 1`. Any crate-prefixed `TypeRef` therefore becomes a dangling reference instead of the newly registered crate id.

`assign_ids` overwrites `local_name_to_id` on every insert, so a type/trait/function short-name collision silently rebinds earlier entries instead of raising `AmbiguousTypeName` as the port contract promises. That can miswire both `TypeRef` resolution and `TraitImplDeclV2` lookup.

`encode_trait_impl_blocks` emits `Impl` items, but the surrounding struct/enum items only store the inherent impl id (`impls: vec![impl_id]`). The trait-impl ids are never attached anywhere, so consumers walking `Struct::impls` or `Enum::impls` will miss every trait implementation.

`Self` never gets a special case here, so `encode_method_items` will serialize constructors and methods returning `Self` as unresolved markers instead of the owning type. That breaks common APIs that use `Self` in parameter or return position.

Generic-argument handling is lossy here: `AssocType` is rewritten as a plain type argument, while `Const`, `AssocConst`, and `Constraint` are dropped. Valid Rust forms like `Iterator<Item = T>` or `ArrayVec<u8, 32>` will not round-trip to the same `rustdoc_types` shape, so downstream consumers see a different type than the catalogue declared.

External trait impls are encoded with `Path.id = Id(ext_crate_id)`, but `Path.id` is an item id, not a crate id. Because crate ids start at 1, this collides with normal local item ids and can make later `krate.paths` lookups resolve the impl to an unrelated local item instead of treating the trait as external.

Trait methods are emitted with `has_body: true` even though `TraitEntry::methods` are declarations, not implementations. That misclassifies trait-associated functions in the rustdoc graph and can confuse any consumer that uses `has_body` to distinguish signatures from concrete methods.

`Path.id` is an item id in rustdoc JSON, but this branch stores the external crate id (`std_crate_id`) for std-prelude paths and the crate id for crate-prefixed paths. Those ids can collide with real local item ids and cause downstream lookups to resolve the wrong item instead of the referenced external type.

External trait impls are encoded with the sentinel `UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID` and no matching `paths` entry is created. Downstream consumers recover trait origin through `krate.paths[trait_id]`, so these impls will lose their origin crate and become effectively anonymous.

`TypeKindV2::Struct` is treated as `fields` only: the `pattern` is discarded at the match site, and `encode_struct` always emits `StructKind::Plain`. That means supported catalogue inputs like `CompositePattern::Newtype { .. }` and `TypestateState { .. }` are encoded as empty/plain structs, losing the declared shape of the type.

`resolved_path_type()` builds `Impl.for_` with an empty `path` string for every inherent/trait impl target. Downstream consumers that format or inspect impl targets by path will see a blank type name, so the owning type is effectively lost in the emitted rustdoc graph.

`convert_generic_args()` is lossy for several valid Rust forms: `Const` arguments are downgraded to an unresolved marker, while `AssocConst` and `Constraint` are dropped entirely. A `TypeRef` that uses const generics or higher-order constraints will not round-trip to the same `rustdoc_types` shape.

`encode_trait_impl_blocks` resolves the trait item by short name alone and ignores `origin_crate`. If the crate defines a local item with the same name as an external trait (for example `Display` while also implementing `std::fmt::Display`), this branch will pick the local `Id` and emit an `Impl` that points at the wrong trait, while never registering the external trait path.

The std-prelude allowlist is checked before local catalogue resolution, so a catalogue type or trait named like a prelude item (`Vec`, `Result`, `String`, etc.) is silently encoded as `std::...` instead of the local item. There is no validation in this scope preventing those names, so this produces incorrect `TypeRef` resolution and downstream graph data.

The `syn` parser is only partially implemented, so valid Rust type syntax is silently mangled instead of preserved. `convert_type` falls back to `<unknown_type>` for `dyn Trait`, `impl Trait`, function pointers, parenthesized types, and qualified paths, and `convert_generic_arg` drops `_`, `AssocConst`, and `Constraint` arguments entirely. That means a syntactically valid `TypeRef` can be encoded into a different type with no error, which is a correctness bug for the codec.

`encode_method_items` inserts method `Function` items into `index` but never registers them in `Crate::paths`. The codec therefore emits an incomplete `paths` table for every inherent/trait method, unlike top-level functions and other in-crate items. Any downstream consumer that resolves canonical names or provenance through `paths` will not be able to find methods.

`convert_type_path` treats every multi-segment `syn::TypePath` as an external crate prefix, and it turns any `qself` path into `<qualified_path>`. That mis-encodes valid Rust type refs such as `crate::foo::Bar`, `self::Bar`, `super::Bar`, and associated types like `<T as Trait>::Assoc` / `Iterator::Item` as unresolved markers or fake external crates, which will corrupt `external_crates` and drop type information.

`array_len_to_string` only preserves integer literals; any const-generic array length expression is collapsed to `<const_len>`. `rustdoc_types::Type::Array.len` is supposed to retain the stringified expression, so `[u8; N]`, `[u8; N + 1]`, etc. lose their real length and downstream consumers get incorrect type data.

`dyn Trait` and `impl Trait` bounds bypass all of the identifier-resolution logic used for `Type::Path`: every trait bound is emitted as `Id(u32::MAX)` with a raw path string, so `dyn Clone`, `impl Iterator`, or `impl serde::Serialize` never resolve against the local catalogue, never hit the std-prelude allowlist, and never auto-register external crates.

`Module.items` is built from `state.local_name_to_id.values()` where `local_name_to_id` is a `HashMap`, so the root module's child order is nondeterministic across runs. The same catalogue can therefore serialize with different top-level item ordering, which is flaky for downstream diffs/snapshots.

Const-expression rendering is lossy here: any cast expression is rewritten as `as _`, so valid inputs like `[u8; N as usize]` or `ArrayVec<T, N as usize>` do not round-trip and downstream consumers lose the actual target type.

`CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodec::encode` is a new public API, but this change only adds happy-path coverage. The two error exits (`AmbiguousIdentifier` and invalid `TypeRef`) are not exercised by direct codec tests, so regressions in collision detection or error mapping can slip through.

`STD_PRELUDE_TYPES` are converted to `std::{name}` and tagged with the unresolved sentinel id in both the type-path and trait-bound branches, but many listed entries are not actually defined at that path (`String`, `Vec`, `HashMap`, `Arc`, `Clone`, etc.). That means valid standard-library references are emitted as incorrect paths and become indistinguishable from truly unresolved types.

Associated const generic constraints are serialized with `syn_expr_to_string`, which collapses any expression beyond a literal or simple path to `"<const_expr>"`. Valid inputs such as `Trait<N = A + 1>` or `Trait<LEN = N as usize>` therefore lose their actual value during encoding.

External trait impls are indexed with `path = [origin_crate, trait_name]`, so a `std::fmt::Display` impl becomes `std::Display`. That does not match the canonical def-paths used elsewhere (`type_ref_parser` canonicalizes `Display` to `std::fmt::Display`), so downstream trait-origin lookup will miss or alias std traits.

When `origin_crate` matches the current crate but `local_name_to_id` has no entry, the code allocates a fresh id and never inserts a corresponding trait item or `ItemSummary`. The resulting impl points at a dangling trait reference instead of failing fast.

Bare function pointer parsing drops the source ABI and HRTB lifetimes: `TypeBareFn.abi` is hardcoded to `Abi::Rust`, and `bare_fn.lifetimes` is ignored, so valid `extern "C" fn` / `for<'a> fn` TypeRef strings are misrepresented in the output type graph.

Generic associated-item metadata is lost during parsing. The `Constraint` variant is discarded, and both `AssocType`/`AssocConst` always set `args: None`, so valid forms like `Iterator<Item: Clone>` or `Trait<Assoc<'a> = T>` lose information instead of being encoded into `AssocItemConstraint` data.

`encode_function` drops the crate component from `FunctionPath`: `register_path` always uses `self.crate_name` and `crate_id: 0`, so any cross-workspace function key (`FunctionPath::crate_name`) will be flattened into the current crate and serialized under the wrong path. This should either validate `fn_path.crate_name == doc.crate_name` or preserve the embedded crate name when building `Crate::paths`.

Free-function paths are inserted into `local_name_to_id`, and that map is later used by `resolve_local` for single-segment `TypeRef`s. A root-level function named like a type or prelude item (for example `String` or `Clone`) can therefore be selected as the resolution target, producing a `Path.id` that points at a function item instead of a type/trait. The same shared map also turns otherwise valid type/function name sharing into false `AmbiguousIdentifier` errors.

`Self` is special-cased before the local-name lookup, so a valid catalogue declaration named `Self` can never be referenced: every `TypeRef` with that spelling is forced to the `Id(0)` self placeholder. Since the identifier layer does not reject `Self`, this silently mis-encodes valid input.

Cross-workspace functions are still stamped with `crate_id: 0` when the `Function` item is inserted. `register_path_for_crate(...)` records the right external crate in `paths`, but `Item::crate_id` remains local, so consumers that filter on `crate_id` will misclassify external functions as part of the current crate.

The `std` trait path fallback guesses `std::{name}` for any trait not in `std_canonical_path`'s hardcoded table. That produces wrong definition paths for valid std traits outside the list, such as `std::error::Error` or `std::io::Read`, which then breaks trait-origin resolution and any downstream path comparisons.

`TypeRef` values that are explicitly resolved as std-prelude or crate-prefixed external types are encoded with `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`, the same marker used for genuinely unknown identifiers. ADR D11/CN-10 says std allowlist and crate-prefixed refs are resolved and must not become Phase 1 unresolved-type errors; only allowlist-outside unknowns should. Because these resolved externals have no distinct id or `paths` entry tying them to `external_crates`, downstream closed-world validation cannot reliably distinguish valid `std::...`/`domain_core::...` references from typos without string-special-casing. Allocate/link a synthetic external item id with a `paths` summary, or use a separate unresolved representation.

`resolve_external_type_ids` only rewrites generic arguments when the `ResolvedPath` itself still has `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`. For a local generic such as `Wrapper<serde::Value>`, the outer `Wrapper` resolves to a local id, so the match falls through and the nested external `serde::Value` keeps the unresolved sentinel even though `serde` was registered. Phase 1 will then reject a valid crate-prefixed TypeRef as undeclared. Recurse into `p.args` for every `ResolvedPath`, not only unresolved ones.

The catch-all arm explicitly passes through `DynTrait` and `FunctionPointer`, but the parser can produce external paths inside both forms, for example `dyn Clone`, `dyn serde::Serialize`, or `fn(serde::Value) -> String`. Those nested paths remain at `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)` after external-crate registration, so closed-world validation will treat valid std/external references as unresolved. Add recursive fix-up for `Type::DynTrait` and `Type::FunctionPointer` contents.

`identifiers.rs` is 854 lines long, which exceeds the repository's 700-line hard limit for a module. The extra size is mostly from co-locating the large test block with the implementation, so this already violates the stated module-size rule and makes future changes harder to keep coherent.

`ModulePath::from_identifiers` is a new public constructor, but there is no unit test covering its happy path. That leaves a public API path in this module without the required test coverage, so a regression in this constructor would not be caught by the current suite.

`ItemAction` derives `strum::Display`/`EnumString` without a rename policy, so it accepts/emits `Add`, `Modify`, etc. The inherited TDDD-03 catalogue contract uses lowercase tags (`add`, `modify`, `reference`, `delete`), and the existing codec/fixtures use those values. Unless this enum is renamed with `#[strum(serialize_all = "snake_case")]` (and covered by tests), valid catalogue actions will be rejected or re-emitted with the wrong spelling once the v2 codec maps through these domain strings.

`SelfReceiver` currently parses/displays the Rust variant names (`Owned`, `SharedRef`, `ExclusiveRef`), but ADR D8 defines the catalogue values as the receiver tokens `self`, `&self`, and `&mut self`. This makes the domain `FromStr` reject the receiver strings already used in catalogue data and will force every caller to duplicate a custom mapping instead of using the validated domain enum. Add explicit strum serializations for the three variants and tests for the token forms.

`Layer` also derives default strum casing, so `Layer::Domain.to_string()` is `Domain` and parsing `domain` fails. ADR D1 defines the layer axis as the lowercase catalogue values `domain` / `usecase` / `infrastructure`; with the current implementation, valid v2 documents using the specified layer values cannot round-trip through the domain enum. Add the appropriate strum serialization policy and tests for the lowercase spellings.
